caveats: a: BLS data cover NAICS 335 rather than 335931 or the outsourced subset.
a: ILO exposure is technical potential rather than observed substitution.
a: Existing automated stamping, molding, assembly, and electrical testing are excluded from the remaining opportunity.
rho: No source measures five-year AI implementation among eligible wiring-device manufacturers.
rho: Certification and safety-critical applications may require conservative human review even when models perform well.
e: The provided firm count is margin-bridged and estimated before applying the outsourced-service lens.
e: A customized configuration of a branded standard device may not constitute outsourced manufacturing.
s5: Owner-age data are cross-industry, based on 2018, and count responding owners rather than firms.
s5: Age does not establish sale intent, transferability, or transaction completion.
q: No public contract dataset establishes benefit sharing for eligible firms.
q: Retention can be lower in standardized outlets and switches than in qualified custom bus bars or terminals.
d5: Electricity demand is an indirect driver and not a direct measure of wiring-device quantity.
d5: BLS projections cover a broader electrical-equipment group.
d5: Residential receptacles, industrial bus bars, and protective devices have different cycles.
o: Operator requirement can persist while production moves offshore or to larger suppliers.
o: Demand elimination and imports are reflected primarily in d5.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers buy recurring production programs under electrical, dimensional, material, safety, testing, certification, packaging, and delivery specifications through purchase orders or supply agreements.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code includes branded standard products and customer-specific contract programs. The lens retains recurring outsourced and private-label production so eligibility and commercial retention describe a coherent model.
lens.excluded_activities: Manufacturers selling only proprietary standardized catalog products, captive plants, electronic connectors classified elsewhere, noncurrent-carrying devices, pure distributors, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market manufacturers that repeatedly produce customer-specific, private-label, or qualified plugs, terminals, bus bars, receptacles, GFCIs, switches, lamp holders, and related current-carrying wiring devices for external OEM, distribution, and infrastructure customers.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for all electrical-equipment manufacturing rather than eligible 335931 suppliers, and ILO reports global GenAI exposure by broad occupation. I assign higher exposure to quoting, engineering documentation, purchasing, scheduling, test-data review, quality and certification records, customer service, and administration and lower exposure to stamping, molding, plating, winding, assembly, testing execution, and material handling. The broad BLS mix may misstate this product mix but demonstrates heavy assembly and inspection employment; GenAI-only evidence may understate machine-vision potential.
caveats: BLS data cover NAICS 335 rather than 335931 or the outsourced subset.
ILO exposure is technical potential rather than observed substitution.
Existing automated stamping, molding, assembly, and electrical testing are excluded from the remaining opportunity.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from at least ten eligible wiring-device suppliers, separating already automated operations from current manual work in engineering, production, quality, certification, and administration, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: BLS reports 65,350 electrical and electronic equipment assemblers, 57,660 team assemblers, and 14,050 inspectors in the broader subsector. AI can assist information and analytical workflows, but most direct production and safety testing remain physical and accountable.

d5.bridge: EIA measures U.S. electricity-load growth and BLS projects the broad other-electrical-equipment manufacturing group, not five-year constant-price demand for eligible current-carrying wiring-device services. EIA reports electricity demand grew about 1.7% annually from 2020 through 2025 and identifies data centers as a growth driver, while BLS projects broad electrical-equipment output growth through 2034. I translate electrification and infrastructure demand into modest service growth, widened for construction cycles, imports, design consolidation, and integrated suppliers. Load growth may overstate wiring-device demand if investment concentrates in upstream equipment and understate replacement or safety-upgrade demand.
caveats: Electricity demand is an indirect driver and not a direct measure of wiring-device quantity.
BLS projections cover a broader electrical-equipment group.
Residential receptacles, industrial bus bars, and protective devices have different cycles.
d5.change_evidence: Customer forecasts, bookings, backlog, current import penetration, data-center and construction project pipelines, utility investment, and product-level shipment histories from eligible firms would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Electrical connections, protection, switching, and outlets remain necessary as power use, data centers, electrification, and infrastructure expand. Housing and commercial construction cycles, imports, product miniaturization, and market share shifts create downside.

caveats: The provided firm count is margin-bridged and estimated before applying the outsourced-service lens.
A customized configuration of a branded standard device may not constitute outsourced manufacturing.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm review of the 138 dataset firms using customer drawings, private-label and contract revenue, tooling ownership, branded catalog mix, repeat programs, captive status, and normalized EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: Census defines a product industry spanning bus bars, GFCIs, lamp holders, lightning arrestors, receptacles, and switches rather than a service model. Customer-specific bus bars, terminals, private-label devices, and qualified OEM components can fit the lens, while branded standard products do not.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 335931 ($2,593,095,360) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 335931 ($8,474,513,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $353,561 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 335931); class <5: 73 firms, avg revenue/firm $590,151 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $115,788 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 51 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,039,725 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $400,194 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 46 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,581,239 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $898,839 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 94 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,336,362 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,420,394 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 44 firms, avg revenue/firm $44,212,727 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,674,537 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 38 firms, avg revenue/firm $131,886,605 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $25,876,152 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 138

caveats: Operator requirement can persist while production moves offshore or to larger suppliers.
Demand elimination and imports are reflected primarily in d5.
o.change_evidence: Customer make-versus-buy plans, supplier-consolidation records, solid-state or integrated-device redesigns, additive manufacturing qualifications, and evidence of fully autonomous certified production would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Remaining demand requires physical fabrication, assembly, insulation, plating, testing, certification, packaging, and accountable electrical-safety conformance. Software can reduce engineering and documentation work but cannot supply the device; displacement is mainly insourcing, supplier consolidation, imports, or product integration.

caveats: No public contract dataset establishes benefit sharing for eligible firms.
Retention can be lower in standardized outlets and switches than in qualified custom bus bars or terminals.
q.change_evidence: A contract and purchase-order sample showing term, rebid cadence, commodity pass-through, open-book provisions, cost-down clauses, tooling ownership, certification responsibility, and historical concessions would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Qualification, certification, tooling, electrical reliability, and delivery performance can preserve savings in customer programs. Annual bids, should-cost models, copper and brass pass-through, distributor leverage, private-label procurement, and import competition transfer visible productivity gains to customers.

caveats: No source measures five-year AI implementation among eligible wiring-device manufacturers.
Certification and safety-critical applications may require conservative human review even when models perform well.
rho.change_evidence: Plant pilots measuring quote time, engineering review, schedule adherence, scrap, false test calls, certification-document hours, accepted recommendations, and integration cost would materially change implementation.
rho.rationale: Quoting, scheduling, procurement, document control, test analytics, and quality workflows can be implemented within five years. Product-safety certification, customer change control, cyber risk, legacy equipment, fragmented test data, and liability for electrical failures constrain deployment.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 51% of responding owners of U.S. employer businesses across industries were age 55 or older, not qualifying wiring-device control transfers. I use this only as succession pressure and reduce it for the five-year window, internal succession, non-sale exits, multi-owner or institutional firms, certification transfer, product liability, customer concentration, and nontransferable operations. The cross-industry proxy may overstate sale probability for scaled branded manufacturers but understate succession pressure in founder-led component suppliers.
caveats: Owner-age data are cross-industry, based on 2018, and count responding owners rather than firms.
Age does not establish sale intent, transferability, or transaction completion.
s5.change_evidence: Closed electrical-component deals, UCC and ownership changes, certification-file transfers, and interviews with sector brokers, owners, and lenders over the prior five years would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Founder succession can create seller supply, but completed transfers require portable customer programs, safety certifications, effective quality systems, defensible intellectual property, maintained tooling, and reduced owner dependence.

principal_driver: Electrification and recurring qualified customer programs support durable demand and improvable engineering, planning, and quality workflows.
principal_weakness: Safety-critical physical production and a small eligible service subset limit implementable AI opportunity.
